content.text: is a dense numeric vector that represents text, images, or other data in a high-dimensional space where semantic similarity maps to geometric proximity. Every time an AI agent performs a retrieval step, it converts a query or document chunk into such a vector and then searches a vector store for the closest neighbours.

stores the mapping from a piece of text to its computed embedding vector so that subsequent requests for the same text are answered from the cache instead of repeating the embedding model call. The cache is keyed on a content hash of the normalised input text and the model identifier, and its value is the raw vector (typically a list of 1 536 or 3 072 float32 values).

Because embedding models are deterministic — the same model always produces the same vector for the same input — caching is lossless: a cached result is bit-for-bit identical to a freshly computed one. This makes embedding caching one of the safest and highest-ROI optimisations in an agentic AI system.

Agentic systems retrieve context frequently. A research agent running 20 retrieval steps per session, each querying a knowledge base containing 50 000 documents, embeds both the query and often re-embeds recurring reference texts. A RAG pipeline reprocesses the same document corpus on every startup unless embeddings are persisted. A customer-support bot receives the same questions dozens of times per hour.

Without caching, every retrieval step issues a network request to an embedding API (50–200 ms round-trip) and incurs a per-token cost. At scale these add up fast: a corpus of 10 million tokens re-embedded daily costs roughly $200/day with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small at current pricing. Caching reduces this to a one-time cost per unique text, with subsequent accesses taking microseconds from memory or 1–2 ms from Redis.

Latency also compounds: a multi-step agent that embeds a query five times per reasoning loop and waits 100 ms each time burns 500 ms per loop purely on embeddings. Caching cuts that overhead to effectively zero for warm cache hits.
— cache hits return in microseconds (in-memory) or low milliseconds (Redis), versus 50–200 ms for an API call. Perceived agent responsiveness improves substantially.
— embedding API calls are billed per token. Caching eliminates repeat charges for recurring texts (repeated queries, static document chunks, fixed system prompts).
— removing network round-trips frees concurrency budget. The same agent fleet can handle more parallel sessions without hitting embedding API rate limits.
— embedding APIs impose requests-per-minute limits. High-traffic agents hit these limits and must retry with back-off. Caching reduces calls, keeping traffic well under the quota.
— a fully warm cache allows an agent to perform retrieval without any network access to the embedding provider, enabling edge deployments.
— seeding a cache with known embeddings makes retrieval-based tests reproducible without live API calls.

— if you switch embedding models (e.g., from text-embedding-3-small to text-embedding-3-large), cached vectors are incompatible. Old cache entries must be purged and texts re-embedded. Failing to do so silently corrupts retrieval results.
— each embedding vector is 1 536 float32 values = 6 KB. One million cached embeddings consume ~6 GB of RAM or Redis memory. Design your eviction strategy accordingly.
— the first request for every unique text still pays full latency. Systems with extremely diverse queries (e.g., open-ended user questions) have low cache hit rates and see minimal benefit.

— two strings that are semantically identical but differ only in whitespace or casing will miss the cache unless normalised before hashing. Robust normalisation (strip, lowercase, collapse spaces) is required but can introduce edge-case bugs.
— in-process LRU caches are not shared across workers. A fleet of 10 agent workers each maintains its own cold cache unless a shared external store (Redis) is used.

— if the text associated with a key changes (e.g., a knowledge-base document is updated but its hash is reused due to a bug), the cache returns the old vector. Keying on content hash rather than a mutable ID prevents this.
The core algorithm is a look-before-compute pattern applied around every embedding model call:
the input text: strip leading/trailing whitespace, collapse multiple spaces, optionally lowercase. This maximises cache hit rate across superficially different but equivalent strings.
: concatenate the normalised text, the model name, and the model version string, then compute SHA-256 (or MD5 for speed). This key is a fixed-length byte string regardless of text length.
in the cache layer. If a vector is found (a
), return it immediately without calling the embedding API.
, call the embedding API with the normalised text. Parse the response to obtain the float vector.
Write the vector to the cache
(key → vector) with an appropriate TTL or eviction policy.
to the caller. Subsequent calls with the same text hit step 3 and short-circuit immediately.

In a multi-tier architecture, the lookup order is: L1 (in-process dict) → L2 (Redis) → L3 (PostgreSQL/Disk) → embedding API. Each tier is populated on a miss from the next tier, so the fastest layer is warmed progressively.

attrs.alt: Embedding caching architecture: text input flows through a cache key generator to an embedding cache; hits bypass the embedding model API while misses call the API and populate the cache before reaching the vector search step. Three storage tiers (process memory, Redis, PostgreSQL) offer speed/durability trade-offs.

content.text: — customer-support bots, FAQ retrieval, and domain-constrained agents see the same questions repeatedly. Cache hit rates of 60–90% are common.
— if a document corpus changes infrequently (monthly updates or less), all chunk embeddings can be pre-computed once and served from cache or a vector store.
— agents that embed the same query or partial context multiple times within a single reasoning chain benefit immediately.
— batch document processing, nightly ETL jobs, and evaluation harnesses that process the same texts across runs eliminate redundant API calls.
— if your embedding quota is too low for peak traffic, caching is the most effective lever before paying for a higher tier.

: queries are highly unique (open-ended creative prompts), texts change frequently (live news feeds with per-second updates), or the operational cost of cache infrastructure exceeds embedding API savings.
for single-worker or development use; Redis for multi-worker production; PostgreSQL with a bytea column for durable cold storage.
: strip whitespace, collapse spaces, optionally lowercase. Apply before hashing.
. Include the model identifier to survive model upgrades.
function that checks the cache, returns a hit, or calls the API and writes the result back.
: use a TTL that matches your re-embedding cadence. If you retrain or upgrade models monthly, set TTL to 30 days. For static corpora, use no TTL but flush the cache on model upgrades.
: store the model name+version in the key. Upgrading the model naturally produces new keys; old entries are evicted by TTL or explicit purge.
) rather than JSON to save ~6× space and avoid serialisation overhead.
: add a counter for hits and misses. A hit rate below 20% suggests the workload is too diverse for caching to be cost-effective.

import hashlib
import functools
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small"

def _normalise(text: str) -> str:
    """Collapse whitespace; preserve case for embedding fidelity."""
    return " ".join(text.split())

def _cache_key(text: str, model: str) -> str:
    payload = f"{model}:{_normalise(text)}"
    return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()

# In-process LRU: up to 10 000 embeddings held in RAM per worker process.
@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=10_000)
def _fetch_embedding(cache_key: str, text: str, model: str) -> tuple[float, ...]:
    """Actual API call. lru_cache keys on cache_key (the hash), not raw text."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(input=text, model=model)
    return tuple(response.data[0].embedding)  # tuple is hashable (required by lru_cache)

def get_embedding(text: str, model: str = MODEL) -> list[float]:
    """Public interface: normalise, hash, check cache, call API on miss."""
    norm = _normalise(text)
    key = _cache_key(norm, model)
    vec_tuple = _fetch_embedding(key, norm, model)
    return list(vec_tuple)

# Usage
vec = get_embedding("What is retrieval-augmented generation?")
# Second call is served from LRU cache — no API call.
vec2 = get_embedding("What is retrieval-augmented generation?")
assert vec == vec2

# Inspect cache statistics
info = _fetch_embedding.cache_info()
print(f"hits={info.hits}, misses={info.misses}, maxsize={info.maxsize}")

import hashlib
import struct
import redis
import numpy as np
from openai import OpenAI

openai_client = OpenAI()
redis_client = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, db=0)

MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small"
DIM = 1536          # number of floats for text-embedding-3-small
TTL_SECONDS = 30 * 24 * 3600  # 30 days

def _normalise(text: str) -> str:
    return " ".join(text.split())

def _cache_key(text: str, model: str) -> str:
    payload = f"emb:{model}:{_normalise(text)}"
    return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()

def _vec_to_bytes(vec: list[float]) -> bytes:
    """Pack floats as little-endian float32 — 4 bytes each, ~6 KB for 1536 dims."""
    return struct.pack(f"<{len(vec)}f", *vec)

def _bytes_to_vec(raw: bytes) -> list[float]:
    n = len(raw) // 4
    return list(struct.unpack(f"<{n}f", raw))

def get_embedding(text: str, model: str = MODEL) -> list[float]:
    """Check Redis first; call the API only on a miss, then cache the result."""
    norm = _normalise(text)
    key = _cache_key(norm, model)

    # L2 Redis lookup
    cached = redis_client.get(key)
    if cached is not None:
        return _bytes_to_vec(cached)

    # Cache miss — call the embedding API
    response = openai_client.embeddings.create(input=norm, model=model)
    vec = response.data[0].embedding

    # Write to Redis with TTL
    redis_client.setex(key, TTL_SECONDS, _vec_to_bytes(vec))

    return vec

# Usage
v1 = get_embedding("What is a vector database?")
v2 = get_embedding("What is a vector database?")  # served from Redis
print(np.allclose(v1, v2))  # True

A legal-document analysis agent receives hundreds of contract clauses per day. The clause vocabulary is bounded: boilerplate paragraphs (indemnification, limitation of liability, governing law) appear in nearly every contract with minor rewording. The agent needs to embed each clause to retrieve similar precedent from a vector store of past contracts.

Without caching, the agent issues 300–500 embedding API calls per contract (one per clause), costing roughly $0.30 per document and adding 30–60 seconds of pure embedding latency per analysis. Most clauses are near-duplicates across documents.

With a Redis cache keyed on normalised clause text, common boilerplate achieves a 75–85% hit rate. The average latency per clause drops from ~120 ms (API call) to ~2 ms (Redis lookup). Per-document embedding cost falls from $0.30 to under $0.05. The following pattern shows the integration:
Keying on raw text without a model identifier
— upgrading from text-embedding-ada-002 to text-embedding-3-small with the same cache returns old incompatible vectors. Always include the model name and version in the key.
for a 1 536-float list produces a ~12 KB string. Packing as float32 bytes gives ~6 KB and parses 10× faster. Use
— caching "hello world" and "hello  world" (double space) separately wastes cache entries and artificially deflates the hit rate. Always normalise before hashing.
Not setting a TTL in Redis
— without eviction, the cache grows unboundedly and will eventually exhaust Redis memory, triggering OOM errors or disabling writes.
Using a mutable document ID as the cache key
— if a document is updated but its ID stays the same, the cache returns stale embeddings. Key on content hash, not on identifiers that can be reused.
— the OpenAI embeddings API accepts a list of strings and returns a list of vectors. Cache each (text, vector) pair individually; do not cache the batch response as a single blob.

— deploying a cold cache to production and immediately expecting high hit rates will disappoint. Pre-populate the cache by running all known documents through the embedding pipeline before serving live traffic.
Include model name and dimension in the key
. This makes the cache self-documenting and safe across model changes.
: store vectors as packed float32 bytes (
. This is the most compact and fastest representation.
: L1 in-process LRU (10 K entries) → L2 Redis (full working set) → embedding API. L1 catches hot entries without a Redis round-trip; L2 shares state across workers.
: strip, collapse whitespace, and decide whether to lowercase. Log the normalisation function version alongside the model version so you can re-warm the cache when normalisation rules change.
Monitor hit rate as a metric
counters. Alert if the hit rate drops below your expected baseline — this signals new unseen query patterns or a cache misconfiguration.
: before a new agent version goes live, embed all static corpora and write results to Redis. This eliminates the cold-start latency spike at first traffic.
: do not flush the entire cache at once if you have a large corpus. Process documents in batches overnight, writing new vectors under the new model-keyed entries before switching traffic.
The performance characteristics of embedding caching differ strongly by tier:

At high throughput, Redis pipeline mode lets you batch multiple cache lookups into a single round-trip. When checking 100 keys in one Redis pipeline call, total round-trip time is still ~2 ms instead of 200 ms for 100 sequential calls.

def get_embeddings_batch(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    """Batch cache lookup with Redis pipeline, single round-trip for all keys."""
    keys = [_cache_key(_normalise(t), MODEL) for t in texts]
    pipe = redis_client.pipeline(transaction=False)
    for k in keys:
        pipe.get(k)
    results = pipe.execute()  # one round-trip for all keys

    embeddings = []
    miss_indices = []
    for i, (cached, text) in enumerate(zip(results, texts)):
        if cached is not None:
            embeddings.append(_bytes_to_vec(cached))
        else:
            embeddings.append(None)
            miss_indices.append(i)

    if miss_indices:
        miss_texts = [_normalise(texts[i]) for i in miss_indices]
        # Batch embed all misses in one API call
        response = openai_client.embeddings.create(input=miss_texts, model=MODEL)
        store_pipe = redis_client.pipeline(transaction=False)
        for idx, embedding_obj in zip(miss_indices, response.data):
            vec = embedding_obj.embedding
            embeddings[idx] = vec
            store_pipe.setex(keys[idx], TTL_SECONDS, _vec_to_bytes(vec))
        store_pipe.execute()

    return embeddings

For very large corpora (100M+ embeddings), consider a tiered approach where L3 (PostgreSQL with pgvector) serves as both the embedding cache and the vector index. This eliminates the need for a separate vector database while keeping embeddings durable.
Pipeline API, SETEX for TTL, binary-safe values
Function-level memoisation; easy to apply as decorator
Stores float4[] columns; cosine/L2 distance indexes
Wraps any LangChain embedder with any BaseStore cache
In-memory or Redis backend; plugs into index pipelines
Stores vectors; no separate embedding cache layer needed
Store computed vectors by content hash
High — µs to ms vs 50-200 ms
Cache attention KV states for a prefix
Cache LLM responses by query similarity
Very high — skips entire LLM call
Embed entire corpus offline; no runtime embedding

Embedding caching and prompt caching are complementary, not competing. A typical RAG agent benefits from both: embedding caching on the retrieval path, prompt caching on the generation path with a repeated system prompt or retrieved context prefix.

— SHA-256 provides 2^256 space; collisions are computationally infeasible. MD5 is faster (4× throughput) and still practically collision-free for this use case, but SHA-256 is preferred for compliance in regulated environments.

— cache keys derived from PII-containing queries may expose sensitive information in Redis key listings. Consider hashing the full key with a server-side HMAC so keys are opaque to Redis admins who are not authorised to see the underlying text.
) in production. Embedding vectors are non-reversible but may still be sensitive metadata (they reveal what queries were made).
in Redis config to prevent OOM crashes. The cache degrades gracefully to an API call on an evicted entry.

— coordinate cache flushing with deployments. A rolling deploy where some workers use a new model and some use the old model will write incompatible vectors under the same key prefixes if model name is not part of the key. Always version the key scheme.

— embedding caching does not affect the idempotency of the retrieval result as long as the model and normalisation are stable. Verify this assumption when enabling caching in a system that relies on reproducible retrieval for audit purposes.
Embedding caching stores computed text vectors by content hash so that identical inputs skip the embedding API call entirely — returning in microseconds instead of 50–200 ms.

Because embedding models are deterministic, caching is lossless: a cached vector is identical to a freshly computed one, making this one of the safest performance optimisations in an agentic pipeline.
Always include the model name and version in the cache key. Upgrading the embedding model without keying on it will silently return incompatible vectors and corrupt retrieval results.
Layer caches: L1 in-process LRU for hot entries within a single worker, L2 Redis for cross-worker sharing with TTL eviction, L3 PostgreSQL/disk for durable cold storage.
Store vectors as packed float32 bytes (struct.pack), not JSON. Binary encoding is 2× more compact and 10× faster to serialise/deserialise.

Monitor cache hit rate as a first-class metric. A hit rate below 20% indicates query diversity is too high for caching to pay off — consider whether semantic caching or a smaller embedding model is a better fit.
Embedding caching and prompt caching are complementary: apply embedding caching on the retrieval path and prompt caching on the generation path for maximum cost and latency reduction in RAG agents.
